scope: Obstetrics & Gynaecology L1+L2 -> L3 or discard

verdicts.new_stem: A 31-year-old woman attends her GP. She had a Mirena 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system inserted 4 years and 11 months ago for contraception and heavy menstrual bleeding. Insertion was via a private clinic that printed a removal-reminder card stating 'replace at 5 years'. She has had no bleeding for 18 months, no pain, threads visible at the routine 4-yearly check. She is in a stable monogamous relationship, has no plans to conceive for at least 5 more years and would prefer to keep this device as long as possible. She specifically asks whether the device must be replaced this month or whether she can defer. Cycles, partner, BMI and screening history are all unremarkable.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice regarding her current Mirena device?

new_options.text: Replace the device this month because the heavy-menstrual-bleeding indication caps duration at 5 years
new_options.rationale: The HMB indication is licensed for 5 years, but if her current need is contraception (HMB now resolved with amenorrhoea), the contraception licence governs duration. The device does not need replacement at 5 years for contraception (TGA PI Mirena 2023).

new_options.text: Replace the device this month because all LNG-IUS devices share a 5-year contraceptive licence in Australia
new_options.rationale: Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS was extended to 8 years for contraception by the TGA in 2023; Kyleena 19.5 mg remains 5 years. The licences are not shared (TGA PI Mirena; AMH 2024).

new_options.text: Continue the current device for contraception until 8 years from insertion, then replace
new_options.rationale: Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS is now TGA-licensed in Australia for up to 8 years of contraception. Her HMB has resolved with amenorrhoea so the operative licence is contraception. She may safely defer replacement to the 8-year mark (TGA PI Mirena 2023; AMH 2024; Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Continue indefinitely as LNG-IUS devices are effective until menopause once inserted after age 25
new_options.rationale: There is no Australian licence for indefinite LNG-IUS use. A Mirena inserted at or after age 45 may be used until age 55 for endometrial protection on MHT, but that is a different scenario; this patient is 31 (Family Planning Australia; AMH).

new_options.text: Replace with a copper IUD now to take advantage of its 10-year licence
new_options.rationale: Switching to copper offers a longer interval but exposes her to heavier bleeding and removes the HMB benefit she may want preserved. There is no clinical indication to switch when the current LNG-IUS remains licensed for a further 3 years (Family Planning Australia).

verdicts.new_stem: A 24-year-old woman attends a sexual-health clinic 96 hours after a single episode of condom failure. Her cycles are regular at 28 days; today is day 16 (intercourse occurred close to her predicted ovulation). She has a BMI of 33 and uses no regular medications. She declines an intrauterine device today because she is needle-phobic about the speculum/insertion. She has no allergies. She wants the oral option with the lowest failure rate given the 96-hour interval and her BMI, and she accepts that she will need to wait 5 days before resuming hormonal contraception if started on this method.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate oral emergency contraceptive in this scenario?

new_options.text: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg orally as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: Levonorgestrel is licensed only up to 72 hours; she presents at 96 hours, so it is outside its window. Efficacy also declines with BMI above 26 and around ovulation (Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Levonorgestrel 3 mg orally as a single dose now, doubled for BMI
new_options.rationale: Although double-dose levonorgestrel is sometimes used off-label for higher BMI, it remains licensed only to 72 hours; at 96 hours it is outside the evidence-based window regardless of dose (Cochrane; Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Ulipristal acetate 30 mg orally as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: Ulipristal is licensed up to 120 hours, is more effective than levonorgestrel around ovulation, and has less BMI-related fall-off below 35. With the IUD declined, ulipristal is the best oral option at 96 hours (Family Planning Australia; Cochrane).

new_options.text: Yuzpe regimen using combined oral contraceptive pills now
new_options.rationale: The Yuzpe regimen is less effective than levonorgestrel or ulipristal and causes more nausea; it is now reserved for settings where dedicated emergency contraceptives are not available (Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill today and abstain for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Starting the COCP today is for ongoing contraception, not emergency contraception; it provides no rescue effect for the unprotected intercourse 96 hours ago (Family Planning Australia).

verdicts.new_stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 9 weeks presents with three weeks of progressive nausea and vomiting, six to ten episodes daily, unable to keep down most fluids, 3.5 kg weight loss (5%), PUQE score 14. She has tried small frequent meals, ginger and oral pyridoxine 25 mg three times daily without benefit. BP 102/68 with a 15 mmHg postural drop, HR 104, dry mucous membranes, ketones 3+ on urinalysis. Na 134, K 3.3, urea 7.8. TSH normal. Ultrasound: single live intrauterine pregnancy. She has been admitted, IV fluids with thiamine have been commenced and the team is choosing the next antiemetic step.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next antiemetic step?

new_options.text: Add oral doxylamine 12.5 mg with pyridoxine 25 mg four times daily as second-line therapy
new_options.rationale: Pyridoxine monotherapy has failed. RANZCOG and Therapeutic Guidelines recommend stepping up to doxylamine-pyridoxine combination as the next-line therapy before broader antiemetics, since safety data in pregnancy are best established (RANZCOG NVP/HG; eTG Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

new_options.text: Switch directly to oral ondansetron 8 mg three times daily as monotherapy
new_options.rationale: Ondansetron is reserved for cases unresponsive to first- and second-line agents because of small first-trimester risks (orofacial clefts) and maternal QT effects; doxylamine-pyridoxine has not yet been trialled (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

new_options.text: Commence IV methylprednisolone 16 mg three times daily today
new_options.rationale: Corticosteroids are last-line in refractory hyperemesis after failure of multiple antiemetics; using them now skips established earlier rungs of the ladder (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

new_options.text: Discharge home on oral ginger and reassure that symptoms typically improve by 12 weeks
new_options.rationale: She has dehydration, ketonuria, hypokalaemia and >=5% weight loss, meeting criteria for hyperemesis admission; discharge without escalation is unsafe (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

new_options.text: Commence intramuscular prochlorperazine 12.5 mg every 6 hours as monotherapy
new_options.rationale: Phenothiazines may be used adjunctively but doxylamine-pyridoxine is preferred next-line per RANZCOG given the better pregnancy safety profile; IM prochlorperazine monotherapy is not the next step (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

verdicts.new_stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 RhD-negative woman is at 13 weeks and 5 days gestation by certain LMP confirmed on first-trimester ultrasound. She presents with a 48-hour history of fresh red vaginal bleeding which has now settled, and has passed several small clots at home. She is haemodynamically stable. At booking she was confirmed Rh(D) negative with a negative antibody screen, and the screen remains negative today. The previous pregnancy ended in a term vaginal birth without complications and she did not receive routine antenatal anti-D in that pregnancy (declined at the time). TVUS today shows a single live intrauterine pregnancy of 13 weeks 5 days with appropriate growth. The decision is to manage expectantly with follow-up in one week.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the appropriate anti-D immunoglobulin regimen for this presentation in Australian practice?

new_options.text: No anti-D is required as bleeding has settled and antibody screen is negative
new_options.rationale: Threatened miscarriage beyond 12 weeks in an RhD-negative non-sensitised woman is an indication for anti-D regardless of whether bleeding has settled (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Anti-D 250 IU IM as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: 250 IU is the dose for sensitising events before 12 weeks. From 13 weeks onwards, including threatened miscarriage, the recommended dose is 625 IU IM (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Anti-D 625 IU IM as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: From 13 weeks gestation onwards, sensitising events including bleeding in early pregnancy require 625 IU IM anti-D in non-sensitised RhD-negative women. Routine 1500 IU prophylaxis is given at 28 and 34 weeks (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Anti-D 1500 IU IM as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: 1500 IU is the routine antenatal prophylaxis dose given at 28 and 34 weeks; it is not the dose for a sensitising event at 13 weeks (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Withhold anti-D until Kleihauer testing confirms feto-maternal haemorrhage
new_options.rationale: Kleihauer testing is not required for sensitising events before 20 weeks; anti-D must be given within 72 hours regardless. Kleihauer is reserved for events from 20 weeks onwards to guide additional dosing (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

verdicts.new_stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 woman attends her midwife at 35 weeks and 4 days. Her two prior births were uncomplicated term vaginal deliveries; her second neonate developed early-onset GBS sepsis confirmed on blood culture at 18 hours of life and recovered fully after IV benzylpenicillin. This pregnancy has been uncomplicated with normal antenatal serology and OGTT. There has been no preterm labour, no PROM, no fever and no GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy. The local maternity service offers either universal culture-based screening at 35-37 weeks or a risk-factor pathway, and her midwife is planning intrapartum management.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding GBS screening and intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis for this woman?

new_options.text: Collect a low vaginal-anorectal swab now and only give intrapartum prophylaxis if positive
new_options.rationale: A previous infant with invasive GBS disease is a stand-alone indication for intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis regardless of swab result; screening is not required for this woman (RANZCOG; CDC).
source_refs: CDC: Prevention of perinatal group B streptococcal disease

new_options.text: Collect a low vaginal-anorectal swab at 36-37 weeks and decide prophylaxis on the result
new_options.rationale: Even with a swab inside the recommended 35-37 week window, a previous infant with invasive GBS disease still mandates intrapartum prophylaxis irrespective of the result (RANZCOG; CDC).

new_options.text: Do not screen; give intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin in labour regardless of swab status
new_options.rationale: A previous neonate with invasive early-onset GBS disease is an automatic indication for intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis; screening adds no value and could falsely reassure if negative (RANZCOG; CDC).
source_refs: CDC: Prevention of perinatal group B streptococcal disease

new_options.text: Do not screen and do not give intrapartum prophylaxis as she had no GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy
new_options.rationale: GBS bacteriuria in the index pregnancy is one indication but is not the only one. A prior infant with invasive GBS is an independent indication that overrides absence of bacteriuria (RANZCOG; CDC).

new_options.text: Screen on admission in labour using a rapid intrapartum PCR and treat if positive
new_options.rationale: Intrapartum PCR is not the standard pathway in Australia, and again, a previous infant with invasive GBS mandates prophylaxis regardless of the test (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 34-year-old woman has her routine 5-yearly cervical screening test through her GP using clinician-collected liquid-based cytology and HPV. She has had two previous CSTs, both negative for oncogenic HPV. She received the quadrivalent HPV vaccine series at age 14. She has no abnormal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and no pelvic pain. Today's result: HPV oncogenic non-16/18 detected; reflex liquid-based cytology shows possible high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (pHSIL). She is not pregnant.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, what is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Repeat HPV cervical screening test in 12 months
new_options.rationale: The 12-month repeat pathway applies to HPV non-16/18 with negative or pLSIL reflex cytology, not pHSIL or HSIL (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Repeat HPV cervical screening test in 5 years as her prior history is reassuring
new_options.rationale: A positive HPV result with high-grade cytology requires escalation not a return-to-routine interval, regardless of prior screening or vaccination history (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Refer directly for colposcopy
new_options.rationale: Under the NCSP, HPV non-16/18 with pHSIL or HSIL on reflex cytology mandates direct colposcopy referral, the same as any HPV 16/18 result (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Refer directly for diagnostic cone biopsy without colposcopy
new_options.rationale: Cone biopsy is a treatment after colposcopic diagnosis, not a primary diagnostic step from a screening result (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Offer a self-collected vaginal HPV swab in 12 months to confirm the result
new_options.rationale: Self-collection is for routine screening, not for follow-up of a high-grade result; colposcopy is required now (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

verdicts.new_stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her child-health-nurse appointment 6 weeks after an uncomplicated term vaginal birth. She describes 4 weeks of low mood, poor sleep beyond infant needs, anhedonia and intrusive thoughts she is a 'bad mother'. She denies thoughts of self-harm or harm to the baby. She has a supportive partner and is breastfeeding well. There is no history of bipolar disorder, prior psychiatric admissions or postpartum psychosis. The nurse administers the COPE-recommended screening instrument: she scores 16 out of 30 with item 10 (self-harm thoughts) scored 0. There are no language or literacy barriers.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Repeat the EPDS in 2-4 weeks without GP referral as the score is below threshold
new_options.rationale: An EPDS score of 13 or more in the postnatal period meets the threshold for further clinical assessment under COPE guidance; 16 with persistent symptoms is not below threshold (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

new_options.text: Refer to her GP for clinical assessment for postnatal depression within 1-2 weeks
new_options.rationale: An EPDS score >=13 with item-10 zero (no acute self-harm risk) indicates probable depression requiring GP clinical assessment within a short window. Acute escalation is not needed; routine perinatal mental-health follow-up is not sufficient (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

new_options.text: Refer to the emergency department for acute psychiatric assessment today
new_options.rationale: ED referral is reserved for active suicidality, infanticidal ideation, psychotic features or rapid decline; item-10 zero with no risk features does not warrant ED (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

new_options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily today without GP review as it is safe in breastfeeding
new_options.rationale: Antidepressant initiation is a medical decision after diagnostic assessment and informed consent; the child-health nurse should not prescribe without GP review (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

new_options.text: Reassure that this represents normal 'baby blues' and review at the 8-week immunisation visit
new_options.rationale: Baby blues resolve by 2 weeks postpartum; symptoms persisting beyond 4 weeks with EPDS 16 indicate probable postnatal depression, not blues (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

verdicts.new_stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP with 18 months of oligomenorrhoea (cycles 45-90 days), facial and chest hair growth requiring weekly waxing, weight gain of 6 kg over 12 months, and acanthosis nigricans. BMI 29, modified Ferriman-Gallwey 12. Total testosterone is at the upper limit of normal, SHBG low, free androgen index elevated, 17-hydroxyprogesterone normal, TSH and prolactin normal, beta-hCG negative. Pelvic ultrasound is technically suboptimal due to bowel gas: each ovary appears to contain 'multiple small follicles' but follicle count cannot be quantified and ovarian volumes are not reported. Anti-Mullerian hormone is 58 pmol/L (markedly elevated).
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step under the 2023 International Evidence-Based PCOS Guideline?

new_options.text: Diagnose PCOS now on the basis of oligo-anovulation plus clinical and biochemical hyperandrogenism
new_options.rationale: The 2023 guideline confirms that any two of (oligo-anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on USS or elevated AMH) meet criteria in adults; this patient meets the first two so the diagnosis is established without USS or AMH (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

new_options.text: Defer diagnosis until a repeat pelvic ultrasound quantifies follicle count and ovarian volume
new_options.rationale: USS is not required once two other Rotterdam features are present, and the 2023 guideline accepts AMH as an alternative to ultrasound; further imaging adds no diagnostic value here (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

new_options.text: Defer diagnosis as elevated AMH alone is sufficient and other criteria are not required
new_options.rationale: AMH or polycystic morphology in isolation is not diagnostic; two of three Rotterdam-derived features remain required in adults (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

new_options.text: Defer diagnosis until LH:FSH ratio confirms elevated LH consistent with PCOS
new_options.rationale: LH:FSH ratio is not part of the Rotterdam-derived criteria and is not required for diagnosis (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

new_options.text: Defer diagnosis until adrenal hyperplasia is excluded with an ACTH stimulation test
new_options.rationale: 17-hydroxyprogesterone is already normal which adequately excludes non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia; ACTH stimulation is unnecessary here (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old woman attends her GP for contraception. She is a non-smoker, BMI 24, BP 122/76 mmHg, with regular 28-day cycles and a normal cervical screening 12 months ago. Over the past year she has had three episodes of unilateral throbbing headache lasting 24 hours, each preceded by a 20-minute episode of zigzag visual lines in one visual field followed by partial right-sided pins and needles. Headaches are now occurring monthly. There is no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism, ischaemic heart disease, breast cancer or liver disease. She is not breastfeeding. She has heard friends recommend the combined oral contraceptive pill and asks specifically about that option.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in contraceptive counselling?

new_options.text: Commence a combined oral contraceptive pill with 20 mcg ethinyloestradiol to minimise vascular risk
new_options.rationale: Migraine with aura at any dose of ethinyloestradiol is UKMEC category 4 (unacceptable health risk) because of stroke risk. A lower dose does not change the category (UKMEC 2016).

new_options.text: Commence a combined oral contraceptive pill with 30 mcg ethinyloestradiol and levonorgestrel
new_options.rationale: CHC is UKMEC 4 in migraine with aura regardless of progestogen choice; the ischaemic stroke association is driven by oestrogen exposure (UKMEC 2016).

new_options.text: Recommend a progestogen-only or non-hormonal method as combined hormonal contraception is contraindicated
new_options.rationale: Visual aura preceding headache is migraine with aura, UKMEC 4 for all combined hormonal contraception. Progestogen-only methods (POP, DMPA, implant, LNG-IUS) and copper IUD are UKMEC 1-2 and safe alternatives (UKMEC 2016).

new_options.text: Continue current condom-only contraception and only review if she develops new neurological deficit
new_options.rationale: Failure to offer a more reliable method is suboptimal; multiple effective progestogen-only and non-hormonal options remain available (Therapeutic Guidelines).

new_options.text: Refer to neurology before any contraceptive can be safely commenced
new_options.rationale: Migraine with aura is a clinical diagnosis from history; neurology referral is not required to begin a UKMEC 1-2 progestogen-only or non-hormonal contraceptive today (UKMEC 2016; RACGP).

verdicts.discard_reason: Pure recall of Rotterdam criteria for PCOS diagnosis; duplicates obgyn-016 which has been revised to a richer alternative-selection L3. Topic does not support a second distinct L3 question without contrivance.

verdicts.new_stem: A 29-year-old nulligravid woman presents with 7 months of secondary amenorrhoea, intermittent bilateral galactorrhoea and reduced libido. She has had no recent pregnancy or breastfeeding, no headaches and no visual symptoms. She started risperidone 4 mg daily 9 months ago for chronic schizoaffective disorder, prescribed by her psychiatrist, and is responding well with no recent admissions. She takes no other regular medications and uses no recreational drugs. BMI 24, BP 116/70. Visual fields are full to confrontation. Bloods: beta-hCG negative, repeat mid-morning serum prolactin 1900 mIU/L (reference <500) on two occasions one week apart, TSH and free T4 normal, U+Es normal. She is keen to conceive in the next 12 months.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Request urgent MRI pituitary with gadolinium before any further investigation
new_options.rationale: MRI is indicated for unexplained sustained hyperprolactinaemia, but when a dopamine-antagonist antipsychotic is the strongly suspected cause, identification and management of that cause should precede imaging (Endocrine Society 2011).

new_options.text: Liaise with the treating psychiatrist about switching to a prolactin-sparing antipsychotic such as aripiprazole, then re-check prolactin
new_options.rationale: Risperidone is a strong dopamine D2 antagonist and a common cause of pharmacological hyperprolactinaemia. The first step is collaborative medication review with the prescribing psychiatrist; switching to a prolactin-sparing agent (aripiprazole) and re-measuring before imaging avoids unnecessary MRI and identifies the cause (Endocrine Society 2011; eTG Endocrinology).

new_options.text: Commence cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly empirically and reassess prolactin in 6 weeks
new_options.rationale: Dopamine agonists may worsen psychosis in this population and are not first-line when a drug-induced cause is highly likely; medication review is the correct next step (Endocrine Society 2011).

new_options.text: Macroprolactin screen with polyethylene glycol precipitation as the next test
new_options.rationale: Macroprolactin screening is for asymptomatic hyperprolactinaemia. This woman has symptoms (amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, low libido) consistent with biologically active hyperprolactinaemia; the focus should be on the offending drug (Endocrine Society 2011).

new_options.text: Stop risperidone abruptly without psychiatric consultation to allow prolactin to normalise
new_options.rationale: Abruptly stopping antipsychotics without psychiatric input risks relapse and is unsafe; switching requires shared decision-making (Endocrine Society 2011).

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old woman is reviewed 8 days after IVF oocyte retrieval (21 oocytes). She reports 48 hours of worsening abdominal distension, nausea and reduced oral intake. BP 108/64, HR 108, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air. Abdomen distended, tense ascites palpable to the costal margin, mild generalised tenderness without peritonism. Pelvic ultrasound: ovaries 11 cm right and 10 cm left with multiple cysts; large-volume ascites extending above the umbilicus; small right pleural effusion. Bloods: haematocrit 0.48 (baseline 0.38), WCC 13.2, sodium 132, potassium 4.6, creatinine 96 micromol/L (baseline 62), albumin 27 g/L, ALT 78 U/L, urine output 25 mL/h for the past 4 hours.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which classification and management plan best fit her ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome?

new_options.text: Moderate OHSS: discharge home with daily phone review and oral fluids
new_options.rationale: Haematocrit >=0.45, rising creatinine, oliguria, hypoalbuminaemia and ascites above the umbilicus place her into severe OHSS, not moderate; outpatient management is unsafe (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

new_options.text: Severe OHSS: admit for IV crystalloid, VTE prophylaxis with LMWH, strict fluid balance and consider paracentesis
new_options.rationale: Haematocrit 0.48, oliguria, creatinine rise, hypoalbuminaemia, ascites above the umbilicus and pleural effusion meet severe OHSS criteria. Management is admission for IV fluids, prophylactic LMWH (high VTE risk), strict input/output and paracentesis for symptomatic tense ascites (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

new_options.text: Critical OHSS: transfer to ICU now for invasive ventilation and renal replacement
new_options.rationale: Critical OHSS requires anuria, ARDS, thromboembolism or multiorgan failure. She has oliguria not anuria, normal saturations and no thromboembolism; ICU is not yet required (RANZCOG OHSS).

new_options.text: Severe OHSS: admit but give large-volume normal saline boluses until haematocrit normalises
new_options.rationale: Aggressive saline boluses worsen third-spacing in OHSS; balanced fluid strategy with albumin where indicated, plus paracentesis, is preferred (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

new_options.text: Severe OHSS: admit but withhold thromboprophylaxis until haemoconcentration resolves
new_options.rationale: OHSS is a hypercoagulable state and LMWH thromboprophylaxis is recommended early; withholding it increases life-threatening VTE risk (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

verdicts.new_stem: A 38-year-old woman and her 40-year-old male partner have been trying to conceive for 18 months. The woman has regular ovulatory cycles, mid-luteal progesterone confirms ovulation, AMH 6 pmol/L (low for age), antral follicle count 6, TSH and prolactin normal, BMI 24, HyCoSy confirmed bilateral tubal patency. The partner has had two normal semen analyses by WHO 2021 criteria 4 weeks apart. Neither has had pelvic surgery, endometriosis or STI. They are non-smokers and drink within recommended limits. They have not had any prior pregnancies. They ask which ART pathway gives the highest live-birth chance given her age and ovarian reserve.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Continue timed intercourse for a further 12 months before referral to a fertility specialist
new_options.rationale: Female age >=35 with infertility >=6-12 months warrants prompt referral; an additional 12 months reduces cumulative live-birth chance, particularly with low AMH (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

new_options.text: Proceed to in vitro fertilisation given her age and low ovarian reserve
new_options.rationale: At 38 with AMH 6 pmol/L and AFC 6 (low ovarian reserve), IVF gives the highest cycle live-birth chance and allows aneuploidy management; IUI is less effective in this context (NICE CG156; RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Three cycles of intrauterine insemination with clomiphene before considering IVF
new_options.rationale: IUI offers limited additional benefit over expectant management in unexplained infertility with low ovarian reserve at 38; cycles spent on IUI may reduce her IVF window (NICE CG156).

new_options.text: Commence clomiphene citrate alone with timed intercourse for six cycles
new_options.rationale: She is already ovulating; clomiphene without IVF or IUI does not address her age-related and reserve-related decline (NICE CG156).

new_options.text: Recommend donor oocyte IVF as her age and reserve preclude autologous IVF success
new_options.rationale: Autologous IVF still offers reasonable success at 38 with AMH 6; donor oocyte is reserved for those with very low reserve and repeated IVF failure (RANZCOG; NICE).

verdicts.new_stem: A 38-year-old G2P2 woman has 9 months of progressively heavy menstrual bleeding and intermenstrual spotting. Hb 102 g/L (microcytic) responsive to iron, TSH and beta-hCG normal, endometrial biopsy benign, cervical screening up to date. Pelvic ultrasound and saline-infusion sonography demonstrate a single 3 cm fibroid lying entirely within the endometrial cavity on a narrow stalk (FIGO type 0), with no other fibroids and a normal myometrium. She has completed her family, wishes to preserve her uterus, and prefers the most targeted intervention with the fastest return to normal activity. She has no contraindication to anaesthesia.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate treatment for her fibroid-related heavy menstrual bleeding?

new_options.text: Hysteroscopic resection of the submucosal fibroid
new_options.rationale: A FIGO type 0 submucosal fibroid causing HMB is best treated by hysteroscopic resection: targeted, uterus-preserving, day-procedure, with rapid recovery and high HMB resolution rates (RANZCOG fibroids; FIGO PALM-COEIN).

new_options.text: Uterine artery embolisation by interventional radiology
new_options.rationale: UAE is effective for symptomatic intramural or larger fibroids but is suboptimal for an isolated cavity-based pedunculated fibroid (FIGO 0), which may necrose into the cavity; hysteroscopic resection is preferred (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena) insertion at next routine appointment
new_options.rationale: LNG-IUS efficacy is reduced when a submucosal fibroid distorts the cavity, and expulsion rates are higher; hysteroscopic resection should precede or replace LNG-IUS in FIGO 0 lesions (RANZCOG; NICE).

new_options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy as definitive treatment
new_options.rationale: She wishes to preserve her uterus and the lesion is well suited to a uterus-sparing day procedure; hysterectomy is disproportionate (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Three-month course of GnRH agonist (goserelin) as definitive management
new_options.rationale: GnRH agonists are used pre-operatively to shrink fibroids and treat anaemia but are not definitive; bleeding returns once stopped, and adverse effects limit long-term use (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 27-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of thin grey-white vaginal discharge with a fishy odour, worse after intercourse. She has no vulval itch, no dysuria, no abnormal bleeding and no fever. She is in a monogamous relationship of 18 months using a copper IUD, last cervical screening 14 months ago was normal. Speculum: thin homogeneous off-white discharge coating the vaginal walls without inflammation. Vaginal pH at the lateral wall 5.2; KOH whiff test positive; wet mount shows clue cells, scant lactobacilli, no motile trichomonads, no pseudohyphae. She is not currently pregnant. She had a similar episode 5 months ago which was treated with a 7-day course of oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily; she completed the course, and symptoms resolved for 4 months before recurring.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Repeat the same 7-day oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily course
new_options.rationale: This is recurrent BV (>=3 episodes in 12 months or symptomatic recurrence after a complete treatment). Simply repeating the standard course alone will not address the high recurrence rate; suppressive maintenance therapy after re-induction is recommended (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

new_options.text: Treat with oral metronidazole 400 mg bd for 7 days, then commence intravaginal metronidazole gel twice weekly for 4-6 months as suppressive therapy
new_options.rationale: Recurrent BV in Australia is managed with re-induction (oral or intravaginal metronidazole) followed by intravaginal metronidazole or clindamycin twice weekly as suppression for several months to reduce recurrence (Australasian STI Guidelines; eTG).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

new_options.text: Reassure that this is normal discharge and no treatment is needed
new_options.rationale: Amsel criteria are met (homogeneous discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells). Symptomatic BV warrants treatment; reassurance alone is inappropriate (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

new_options.text: Empirically treat both her and her male partner with metronidazole
new_options.rationale: Routine treatment of male partners of women with recurrent BV is not currently standard in Australian guidelines, although the evidence base is evolving. Suppressive therapy for the index patient is the established next step (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

new_options.text: Remove the copper IUD as it is the underlying cause of recurrent BV
new_options.rationale: The copper IUD is not a clear cause of BV and removal is not first-line; suppressive therapy is preferred before considering device change (Family Planning Australia).

verdicts.new_stem: A 21-year-old woman at 18 weeks of pregnancy presents with multiple soft non-keratinised fleshy lesions on the vulva and perianal area, first noticed 6 weeks ago and increasing in number. She is otherwise well with an uncomplicated pregnancy on midwifery shared care. She did not receive the HPV vaccine in school. On examination there are approximately 10 papillomatous lesions 2-5 mm distributed over the labia majora and perianal area; no internal vaginal, cervical or intra-anal lesions; cervical screening normal 4 months ago. She is keen to treat them before delivery and prefers home application.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her anogenital warts?

new_options.text: Self-applied podophyllotoxin 0.5% solution twice daily for 3 days, then 4 days off, repeated up to 4 cycles
new_options.rationale: Podophyllotoxin is contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenicity (Australasian STI Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

new_options.text: Self-applied imiquimod 5% cream three nights per week for up to 16 weeks
new_options.rationale: Imiquimod is not recommended in pregnancy because of limited safety data; podophyllotoxin and imiquimod should both be avoided (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

new_options.text: Clinician-applied cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen weekly
new_options.rationale: Cryotherapy is safe in pregnancy and is the preferred first-line treatment for anogenital warts in pregnant women in Australian guidelines; trichloroacetic acid is an alternative. Self-applied options are contraindicated (Australasian STI Guidelines; eTG).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

new_options.text: Watch and wait until after delivery as warts always regress postpartum
new_options.rationale: Warts may proliferate during pregnancy and can grow large; treatment is offered antenatally to reduce burden at delivery, not deferred indefinitely (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

new_options.text: Plan elective caesarean section solely to prevent vertical HPV transmission
new_options.rationale: Caesarean is not indicated solely for HPV transmission risk; vaginal delivery is acceptable unless warts obstruct the birth canal (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts
